title: Lesson Quiz: Understanding Paychecks and Deductions
description: Assessment of the concept that Gross vs. net income, taxes, and deductions.

choices.feedback: That describes gross income, not net income.

choices.text: The total number of hours worked
choices.feedback: Hours worked help determine earnings, but they do not define net income.

choices.text: The amount added to a purchase after sales tax
choices.feedback: Net income refers to earnings after deductions, not purchase totals.

choices.text: The amount taken home after deductions
choices.feedback: Correct. Net income is the take-home pay after deductions.

questions.prompt: Gross income is usually higher than net income because deductions are removed later.

choices.feedback: Gross income is usually higher because deductions reduce the final amount.
Correct. Deductions lower the take-home amount, which is net income.

questions.prompt: A worker earns $18 per hour for 10 hours and has $25 in deductions. What is the net income?

choices.feedback: This net income is too low. Find the gross income first and then subtract the deductions.
$180 is the gross income before deductions.
Correct. Gross income is $180, and $180 minus $25 equals $155.
$205 would be too high after deductions.

questions.prompt: Why should a person use net income instead of gross income when planning spending?

choices.text: Because gross income is never important
choices.feedback: Gross income is still important, but it is not the spendable amount.

choices.text: Because net income is the actual amount available after deductions
choices.feedback: This is a strong reason, but the correct answer in this set states the planning reason more fully.

choices.text: Because net income is the real take-home amount that can be used for actual financial decisions
choices.feedback: Correct. Spending plans should be based on the actual amount a person keeps.

choices.text: Because deductions only matter for employers
choices.feedback: Deductions matter for workers because they change take-home pay.

questions.prompt: Deductions can reduce the amount of money a worker has available to spend.

choices.feedback: Correct. Deductions reduce take-home pay and affect actual financial options.
Deductions do affect the amount of money available after a paycheck is received.
